label: ch20theoremI
prose: Let \(\langle a, b \rangle \) be an inner product on a real vector space \(V\) (with the norm \(|a|^2 := \langle a, a \rangle \)). Then \[ \langle a, b \rangle ^2 \leq |a|^2 |b|^2 \] holds for all vectors \(a, b \in V\), with equality if and only if \(a\) and \(b\) are linearly dependent.

label: ch20theoremIIproof1
prose: Let \(a_1, \dots a_n\) be positive real numbers, then \[ \frac{n}{\frac{1}{a_1}+\dots +\frac{1}{n_n}} \le \sqrt[n]{a_1a_2\dots a_n} \le \frac{a_1\dots a_n}{n} \] with equality in both cases if and only if all \(a_i\)’s are equal.

label: ch20theoremIIproof2
prose: Let \(a_1, \dots a_n\) be positive real numbers, then \[ \frac{n}{\frac{1}{a_1}+\dots +\frac{1}{n_n}} \le \sqrt[n]{a_1a_2\dots a_n} \le \frac{a_1\dots a_n}{n} \] with equality in both cases if and only if all \(a_i\)’s are equal.

label: ch20theoremIIproof3
prose: Let \(a_1, \dots a_n\) be positive real numbers, then \[ \frac{n}{\frac{1}{a_1}+\dots +\frac{1}{n_n}} \le \sqrt[n]{a_1a_2\dots a_n} \le \frac{a_1\dots a_n}{n} \] with equality in both cases if and only if all \(a_i\)’s are equal.

label: ch20theorem1
prose: Suppose all roots fo the polynomial \(x^n + a_{n - 1}x^{n - 1} + \dots +a_0\) are real. Then the roots of are contained in the interval with the endpoints \[ -\frac{n_{n-1}}{n}\pm \frac{n - 1}{n}\sqrt{a^n_{n - 1} - \frac{2n}{n - 1}a_{n - 2}}. \]

label: ch20theorem2
prose: Let \(f(x)\) be a real polynomial of degree \(n \ge 2\) with only real roots, such that \(f(x){\gt} 0\) for \( -1 {\lt} x {\lt} 1\) amd \(f(-1) = f(1) = 0\). Then \[ \frac{2}{3}T \le A \le \frac{2}{3}R, \] and equality holds in both cases only for \(n=2\).

label: ch20theorem3proof1
prose: Suppose \(G\) is a graph on \(n\) vertices without triangles. Then \(G\) has at most \(\frac{n^2}{4}\) edges, and equality holds only when \(n\) is even and \(G\) is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{n/2, n/2}\).

label: ch20theorem3proof2
prose: Suppose \(G\) is a graph on \(n\) vertices without triangles. Then \(G\) has at most \(\frac{n^2}{4}\) edges, and equality holds only when \(n\) is even and \(G\) is the complete bipartite graph \(K_{n/2, n/2}\).

label: pigeon_hole_principle
prose: If \(n\) objects are placed in \(r\) boxes, where \(r {\lt} n\), then at least one of the boxes contains more than one object.

label: ch28claim1
prose: Consider the numbers \(1, 2, 3, \dots , 2n\), and take any \(n + 1\) of them. Then there are two among these \(n + 1\) numbers which are relatively prime.

label: ch28claim2
prose: Suppose again \(A \subset \{ 1, 2, \dots , 2n\} \) with \(|A| = n+1\). Then there are always two numbers in \(A\) such that one divides the other.

label: ch28claim3
prose: In any sequence \(a_1, a_2, \ldots , a_{mn+1}\) of \(mn+1\) distinct real numbers, there exists an increasing subsequence \[ a_{i_1} {\lt} a_{i_2} {\lt} \cdots {\lt} a_{i_{m+1}} \quad (i_1 {\lt} i_2 {\lt} \cdots {\lt} i_{m+1}) \] of length \(m+1\), or a decreasing subsequence \[ a_{j_1} {\gt} a_{j_2} {\gt} \cdots {\gt} a_{j_{n+1}} \quad (j_1 {\lt} j_2 {\lt} \cdots {\lt} j_{n+1}) \] of length \(n+1\), or both.

label: ch28claim4
prose: Suppose we are given \(n\) integers \(a_1, \dots , a_n\), which need not be distinct. Then there is always a set of consecutive numbers \(a_{k+1}, a_{k+2}, \dots , a_{\ell }\) whose sum \(\sum _{i=k+1}^{\ell } a_i\) is a multiple of \(n\).

label: double_counting
prose: Suppose that we are given two finite sets \(R\) and \(C\) and a subset \(S \subseteq R \times C\). Whenever \((p, q) \in S\), then we say \(p\) and \(q\) are incident. If \(r_p\) denotes the number of elements that are incident to \(p \in R\), and \(c_q\) denotes the number of elements that are incident to \(q \in C\), then \[ \sum _{p \in R} r_p = |S| = \sum _{q \in C} c_q. \tag {3} \]

label: ch28_avg_divisors
prose: For any positive integer \(n\), \[ \sum _{j=1}^{n} t(j) = \sum _{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{n}{i} \right\rfloor . \] Consequently, \[ \log n - 1 {\lt} \bar{t}(n) \leq H_n {\lt} \log n + 1, \] where \(H_n = \sum _{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i}\) is the \(n\)-th harmonic number.

label: handshaking
prose: Let \(G\) be a finite simple graph with vertex set \(V\) and edge set \(E\) and let \(d(v)\) denote the degree of a vertex \(v\). Then \[ \sum _{v\in V}d(v) = 2|E|. \tag {4} \]

label: ch28theorem
prose: If the graph \(G\) on \(n\) vertices contains no \(4\)-cycles, then \[ |E| \leq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{4}\left(1 + \sqrt{4n - 3}\right) \right\rfloor . \tag {6} \]

label: ch28_sum_choose
prose: If the graph \(G\) on \(n\) vertices contains no \(4\)-cycles, then \[ \sum _{v \in V} \binom {d(v)}{2} \leq \binom {n}{2}. \]

label: sperner
prose: Suppose that some “big” triangle with vertices \(V_1, V_2, V_3\) is triangulated (that is, decomposed into a finite number of “small” triangles that fit together edge-by-edge). Assume that the vertices in the triangulation get “colors” from the set \(\{ 1, 2, 3\} \) such that \(V_i\) receives the color \(i\) (for each \(i\)), and only the colors \(i\) and \(j\) are used for vertices along the edge from \(V_i\) to \(V_j\) (for \(i \neq j\)), while the interior vertices are colored arbitrarily with \(1\), \(2\), or \(3\). Then in the triangulation there must be a small “tricolored” triangle, which has all three different vertex colors.

label: brouwer
prose: Every continuous function \(f : B^2 \to B^2\) of a \(2\)-dimensional ball to itself has a fixed point (a point \(x \in B^2\) with \(f(x) = x\)).

label: ch30theorem1
prose: The size of a largest antichain of an \(n\)-set is \(\binom {n}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor }\).

label: ch30lemma
prose: Let \(n \ge 2k\), and suppose we are given \(t\) distinct arcs \(A_1, \dots A_t\) of length \(k\) on a circle of \(n\) points, such that any two arcs share at least one point. Then \(t \le k\).

label: ch30theorem2
prose: Let \(n \ge 2k\). The largest size of an intersecting \(k\)-uniform family in an \(n\)-set is \(\binom {n - 1}{k - 1}\).

label: ch30theorem3
prose: Let \(A_1, \dots A_n\) be a collection of subsets of a finite set \(X\). Then there exists a system of distinct representatives if and only if the union of any \(m\) sets \(A_i\) contains at least \(m\) elements, for \(1 \le m \le n\).

label: ch30corollary
prose: Suppose the sets \(A_1, \dots , A_n\) all have size \(k \ge 1\) and suppose further that no element is contained in more than \(k\) sets. Then there exist \(k\) SDR’s such that for any \(i\) the \(k\) representatives of \(A_i\) are distinct and thus together form the set \(A_i\).

label: thm:euclids_proof
prose: A finite set \(\{ p_1, \dots , p_r\} \) cannot be the collection of all prime numbers.

label: thm:second_proof
prose: Any two Fermat numbers \(F_n := 2^{2^n} + 1\) are relatively prime.

label: thm:third_proof
prose: There is no largest prime.

label: thm:fourth_proof
prose: The prime counting function is unbounded

label: thm:fifth_proof
prose: The set of primes \(\mathbb {P}\) is infinite.

label: thm:sixth_proof
prose: The series \(\sum _{p\in \mathbb {P}}\frac1p\) diverges.

label: thm:infty_proof
prose: If the sequence \(S = (s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots )\) is almost injective and of subexponential growth, then the set \(\mathbb {P}_S\) of primes that divide some member of \(S\) is infinite.

label: thm:infinity_of_primes
prose: There are infinitely many primes. (Six + infinitely many proofs)

label: thm:bertrands_postulate
prose: For any positive natural number, there is a prime which is greater than it, but no more than twice as large.

label: thm:estimate_integral
prose: For all \(n \in \mathbb {N}\) \[ \log n + \frac1n {\lt} H_n {\lt} \log n + 1. \]

label: thm:estimate_factorials
prose: For all \(n \in \mathbb {N}\) \[ e\left(\frac{n}{e}\right)^n {\lt} n! {\lt} en\left(\frac{n}{e}\right)^n. \]

label: thm:estimate_binomial_coefficient
prose: \[ \binom {n}{k} \le \frac{n^k}{k!} \le \frac{n^k}{2^{k - 1}} \]

label: sylvester
prose: For all positive natural \(n, k\) such that \(n \ge 2k\), at least one of the numbers \(n, n - 1, \dots , n - k + 1\) has a prime divisor \(p\) greater than \(k\), or, equivalently the binomial coefficient \(\binom {n}{k}\) always has a prime factor \(p {\gt} k\).

label: binomial_never_powers
prose: The equation \(\binom n k = m ^l\) has no integer solutions with \(l \ge 2\) and \(4 \le k \le n - 4\).

label: ch04.lemma₁
prose: For primes \(p = 4m + 1\) the equation \(s^2 \equiv -1 (\mod p)\) has two solutions \(s \in \{ 1, 2, \dots , p - 1\} \), for \(p = 2\) there is one such solution, while for primes of the form \(p = 4m + 3\) there is no solution.

label: ch04.lemma2
prose: No number \(n = 4m + 3\) is a sum of two squares.

label: ch04.proposition1
prose: Every prime of the form \(p = 4m + 1\) is a sum of two squares, that is, it can be written as \(p = x^2 + y^2\) for some natural numbers \(x,y \in \mathbb {N}\).

label: ch04.proposition2
prose: Every prime of the form \(p = 4m + 1\) is a sum of two squares, that is, it can be written as \(p = x^2 + y^2\) for some natural numbers \(x,y \in \mathbb {N}\).

label: ch04.proposition3
prose: Every prime of the form \(p = 4m + 1\) is a sum of two squares, that is, it can be written as \(p = x^2 + y^2\) for some natural numbers \(x,y \in \mathbb {N}\).

label: sum_of_two_squares
prose: A natural number \(n\) can be represented as a sum of two squares if and only if every prime factor of the form \(p = 4m + 3\) appears with an even exponent in the prime decomposition of \(n\).

label: fermats_little
prose: For \(a \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}\), \[ a^{p - 1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \]

label: euler_criterion
prose: For \(a \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}\), \[ (\frac{a}{p}) \equiv a ^{\frac{p-1}{2}} \pmod{p} \]

label: product_rule
prose: \begin{equation} \label{eq:product_rule} (\frac{ab}{p}) = (\frac{a}{p}) \cdot (\frac{b}{p}) \end{equation} 1

label: gauss_lemma
prose: Suppose \(a \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}\). Take the numbers \(1a, 2a, \dots , \frac{p-1}{2}a\) and reduce them modulo \(p\) to the residue system smallest in absolute value, \(ia \equiv r_i \pmod{p}\) with \(-\frac{p-1}{2} \le r_i \le \frac{p-1}{2}\) for all \(i\). Then \[ (\frac{a}{p}) = (-1)^s, \quad \text{where } s = \# \{ i : r_i {\lt} 0\} . \]

label: quadratic_reciprocity1
prose: Let \(p\) and \(q\) be different odd primes. Then \[ (\frac{q}{p})(\frac{p}{q}) = (-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2} \frac{q-1}{2}}. \]

label: mult_cyclic
prose: The multiplicative group of a finite field is cyclic.

label: fact_A
prose: Let \(p\) and \(q\) be distinct odd primes, and consider the finite field \(F\) with \(q^{p-1}\) elements. Then for any \(a, b \in F\), \((a + b)^q = a^q + b^q\).

label: fact_B
prose: For the field \(F\) defined in (A), there exists an element \(\zeta \in F\) of multiplicative order \(p\), that is, \(\zeta ^p = 1\). Moreover, we have a polynomial decomposition \[ x^p - 1 = (x - \zeta ) (x - \zeta ^2) \cdots (x - \zeta ^p). \]

label: quadratic_reciprocity2
prose: Let \(p\) and \(q\) be different odd primes. Then \[ (\frac{q}{p})(\frac{p}{q}) = (-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2} \frac{q-1}{2}}. \]

label: root_of_unity
prose: The \(n\)-th roots of unity are \[ \lambda _k = e^{\frac{2k\pi i}{n}} = \cos (2k\pi /n) + i \sin (2k\pi /n), \quad 0 \le k \le n - 1. \]

label: wedderburn
prose: Every finite division ring is commutative.

label: ch7.lemma
prose: If \(A\) is a real symmetric \(n \times n\) matrix that is not diagonal, that is, \(\operatorname{Od}(A) {\gt} 0\), then there exists \(U \in O(n)\) such that \(\operatorname{Od}(U^T A U) {\lt} \operatorname{Od}(A)\).

label: diagonalize_real_symmetric
prose: For every real symmetric matrix \(A\) there is a real orthogonal matrix \(Q\) such that \(Q^{T}AQ\) is diagonal.

label: thm:hadamard_inequality
prose: For any real \(n \times n\) matrix \(A = (a_{ij})\) with \(|a_{ij}| \le 1\), \[ |\det A| \le n^{n/2}. \]

label: thm:hadamard_order
prose: If a Hadamard matrix of size \(n \times n\) exists for \(n {\gt} 2\), then \(n\) must be a multiple of \(4\).

label: thm:hadamard_existence
prose: Hadamard matrices exist for all \(n = 2^m\).

label: thm:det_greater_n_fact
prose: There exists an \(n \times n\) matrix with entries \(\pm 1\) whose determinant is greater than \(\sqrt{n!}\).

label: e_irrational
prose: \(e\) is irrational.

label: e_pow_2_irrational
prose: \(e^2\) is irrational.

label: little_lemma
prose: For any \(n \ge 1\) the integer \(n!\) contains the prime factor \(2\) at most \(n-1\) times — with equality if (and only if) \(n\) is a power of two, \(n = 2^m\).

label: e_pow_4_irrational
prose: \(e^4\) is irrational.

label: lem_aux_i
prose: For some fixed \(n \ge 1\), let \[ f(x) = \frac{x^n (1-x)^n}{n!}. \] The function \(f(x)\) is a polynomial of the form \(f(x) = \frac{1}{n!} \sum _{i=n}^{2n} c_i x^i\), where the coefficients \(c_i\) are integers.

label: lem_aux_ii
prose: For \(0 {\lt} x {\lt} 1\) we have \(0 {\lt} f(x) {\lt} \frac{1}{n!}\).

label: lem_aux_iii
prose: The derivatives \(f^{(k)}(0)\) and \(f^{(k)}(1)\) are integers for all \(k \ge 0\).

label: book.irrational.Theorem_1
prose: \(e^r\) is irrational for every \(r \in \mathbb {Q} \setminus \{ 0\} \).

label: book.irrational.Theorem_2
prose: \(\pi ^2\) is irrational.

label: book.irrational.Theorem_3
prose: For every odd integer \(n \ge 3\), the number \[ A(n) := \frac{1}{\pi } \arccos \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \] is irrational.

label: four_proofs_euler_series
prose: Collecting the proofs from the chapter.

label: pearl_lemma
prose: If \(P\) and \(Q\) are equidecomposable, then one can place a positive number of pearls (that is, assign positive integers) to all the segments of the decompositions \(P = P_1 \cup \cdots \cup P_n\) and \(Q = Q_1 \cup \cdots \cup Q_n\) in such a way that each edge of a piece \(P_k\) receives the same number of pearls as the corresponding edge of \(Q_k\).

label: cone_lemma
prose: If a system of homogeneous linear equations with integer coefficients has a positive real solution, then it also has a positive integer solution.

label: bricard_condition
prose: TODO

label: example1
prose: TODO

label: example2
prose: TODO

label: example3
prose: TODO

label: hilberts_third
prose: TODO

label: ch11_theorem1
prose: In any configuration of \(n\) points in the plane, not all on a line, there is a line which contains exactly two of the points.

label: ch11_theorem2
prose: Let \(P\) be a set of \(n\ge 3\) points in the plane, not all on a line. Then the set \(\mathcal{L}\) of lines passing through at least two points contains at least \(n\) lines.

label: ch11_theorem3
prose: Let \(X\) be a set of \(n\ge 3\) elements, and let \(A_1, \dots , A_m\) be proper subsets of \(X\), such that every pair of elements of \(X\) is contained in precisely one set \(A_i\). Then \(m\ge n\) holds.

label: ch11_theorem4
prose: If \(K_n\) is decomposed into complete bipartite subgraphs \(H_1, \dots , H_m\), then \(m \ge n - 1\).

label: slope_problem
prose: If \(n\ge 3\) points in the plane do not lie on one single line, then they determine at least \(n - 1\) different slopes, where equality is possible only if \(n\) is odd and \(n \ge 5\).

label: euler_formula
prose: If \(G\) is a connected plane graph with \(n\) vertices, \(e\) edges and \(f\) faces, then \[ n - e + f = 2. \]

label: euler_consequence_a
prose: Let \(G\) be any simple plane graph with \(n{\gt}2\) vertices. Then \(G\) has at most \(3*n - 6\) edges.

label: euler_consequence_b
prose: Let \(G\) be any simple plane graph with \(n{\gt}2\) vertices. Then \(G\) has a vertex of degree at most \(5\).

label: euler_consequence_c
prose: Let \(G\) be any simple plane graph with \(n{\gt}2\) vertices. If the edges of \(G\) are two-colored, then there is a vertex of \(G\) with at most two color-changes in the cyclic order of the edges around the vertex.

label: sylvester_gallai2
prose: Given any set of \(n \ge 3\) points in the plane, not all on one line, there is always a line that contains exactly two of the points.

label: monochromatic_lines
prose: Given any finite configuration of “black” and “white” points in the plane, not all on one line, there is always a “monochromatic” line: a line that contains at least two points of one color and none of the other.

label: pick_lemma
prose: Every elementary triangle \(\Delta = \operatorname{conv}\{ p_0, p_1, p_2\} \subset \mathbb {R}^2\) has area \(A(\Delta )=12\)

label: pick_theorem
prose: The area of any (not necessarily convex) polygon \(Q\subset \mathbb {R}^2\) with integral vertices is given by \[ A(Q) = n_{int} + \frac{1}{2}n_{bd} - 1 \] where \(n_{int}\) and \(n_{bd}\) are the numbers of integral points in the interior respectively on the boundary of \(Q\).

label: arm_lemma
prose: TODO

label: cauchy_rigidity
prose: If two \(3\)-dimensional convex polyhedra \(P\) and \(P'\) are combinatorially equivalent with corresponding pairs of adjacent congruent, then also the angels between corresponding pairs of adjacent facets are equal (and thus \(P\) is congruent to \(P'\)).

label: ch14theorem1
prose: If a link consists of disjoint perfect circles that are pairwise not linked, then the link is trivial

label: borromean_nontrivial
prose: The Borromean rings are nontrivial, and they are also not equivalent to Tait’s link No. 18

label: borromean
prose: The Borromean rings cannot be build from three perfect circles

label: ch16theorem1
prose: For every \(d\ge 2\), there is a family of \(2^d\) pairwise touching \(d\)-simplices in \(\mathbb {R}^d\) together with a transversal line that hits the interior of every single on of them.

label: ch16theorem2
prose: For all \(d \ge 1\), we have \(f(d){\lt} 2^{d+1}\).

label: ch17theorem1
prose: For every \(d\), one has the following chain of inequalities: \begin{align} 2^d & \leq _{\text{(1)}} \max \left\{ \# S \, |\, S \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d, \angle (s_i, s_j, s_k) \leq \frac{\pi }{2} \text{ for every } \{ s_i, s_j, s_k\} \subseteq S \right\} \\ & \leq _{\text{(2)}} \max \left\{ \# S \, |\, S \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ such that for any two points } \{ s_i, s_j\} \subseteq S, \right. \nonumber \\ & \qquad \qquad \left. \text{there is a strip } S(i, j) \text{ that contains } S, \text{ with } s_i \text{ and } s_j \text{ lying in the parallel boundary hyperplanes of } S(i, j) \right\} \\ & =_{\text{(3)}} \max \left\{ \# S \, |\, S \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ such that the translates } P - s_i, s_i \in S, \text{ of the convex hull } P := \text{conv}(S) \right. \nonumber \\ & \qquad \qquad \left. \text{intersect in a common point, but they only touch} \right\} \\ & \leq _{\text{(4)}} \max \left\{ \# S \, |\, S \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ such that the translates } Q + s_i \text{ of some d-dimensional convex polytope } Q \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ touch pairwise} \right\} \\ & =_{\text{(5)}} \max \left\{ \# S \, |\, S \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ such that the translates } Q^* + s_i \text{ of some d-dimensional centrally symmetric convex polytope } Q^* \subseteq \mathbb {R}^d \text{ touch pairwise} \right\} \\ & \leq _{\text{(6)}} 2^d. \end{align}

label: ch17theorem2
prose: For every \(d\ge 2\), there is a set \(S\subset \{ 0, 1\} ^d\) of \(2\lfloor \frac{sqrt{6}}{9}(\frac{2}{\sqrt(3)})^d\rfloor \) points in \(\mathbb {R}^n\) (vertices of the unit \(d\)-cube) that determine only acute angels. In particular, in dimension \(d = 34\) there is a set of \(72 {\gt} 2*34 - 1\) points with only acute angels.

label: borsuk_conjecture
prose: Let \(q = p^m\) be a prime power, \(n := 4q - 2\), and \(d := \binom {n}{2} = (2q - 1)(4q - 3)\). Then there is a set \(S \subseteq \{ +1, -1\} ^d\) of \(2^{n-2}\) points in \(\mathbb {R}^d\) such that every partition of \(S\), whose parts have smaller diameter than \(S\), has at least \[ \frac{2^{n-2}}{\sum _{i=0}^{q-2} \binom {n-1}{i}} \] parts. For \(q = 9\) this implies that the Borsuk conjecture is false in dimension \(d = 561\). Furthermore, \(f(d) {\gt} (1.2)\sqrt{d}\) holds for all large enough \(d\).

label: ch19theorem1
prose: The set of \(\mathbb {Q}\) of rational numbers is countable.

label: ch19theorem2
prose: The set \(\mathbb {R}\) of real numbers is not countable

label: ch19theorem3
prose: The set \(\mathbb {R}^2\) of all ordered pairs of real numbers (that is, the real plane) has the same size as \(\mathbb {R}\).

label: ch19theorem4
prose: If each of two sets \(M\) and \(N\) can be mapped injectively into the other, then there is a bijection from \(M\) to \(N\), that is \(|M| = |N|\).

label: ch19theorem5
prose: If \(c {\gt} \aleph _1\), then every family \(\{ f_\alpha \} \) satisfying \((P_0)\) is countable. If, on the other hand, \(c = \aleph _1\), then there exists some family \(\{ f_\alpha \} \) with property \(P_0\) which has size \(c\).

label: ch19proposition1
prose: Let \(\mu \) be an ordinal number and denote by \(W_\mu \) the set of ordinal numbers smaller than \(\mu \). Then the following holds: The elements of \(W_\mu \) are pairwise comparable. If we order \(W_\mu \) according to their magnitude, then \(W_\mu \) is well-ordered and has ordinal number \(\mu \).

label: ch19proposition2
ch19proposition3
prose: Every set of ordinal numbers (ordered according to magnitude) is well-ordered.

label: ch19proposition4
prose: For every cardinal number \(\mathfrak {m}\), there is a definite next larger cardinal number.

label: ch19proposition5
prose: Let the infinite set \(M\) have cardinality \(\mathfrak {m}\), and let \(M\) be well ordered according to the initial ordinal number \(\omega _{\mathfrak {m}}\). Then \(M\) has no last element.

label: ch19proposition6
prose: Suppose \(\{ A_\alpha \} \) is a family of size \(\mathfrak {m}\) of countable sets \(A_\alpha \), where \(\mathfrak {m}\) is an infinite cardinal. Then the union \(\bigcup _\alpha A_\alpha \) has size at most \(\mathfrak {m}\).

label: argand_inequality
prose: Let \(p(z) = \sum _{k=0}^n c_k z^k\) be a complex polynomial of degree \(n\ge 1\). If \(p(a)\ne 0\), then every disk \(D\) around \(a\) contains an interior point \(b\) with \(|p(b)| {\lt} |p(a)|\)

label: fundamental_theorem_of_algbra
prose: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one root in the field of complex numbers.

label: three_coloring
prose: TODO

label: rainbow_triangle
prose: TODO

label: ch22lemma1
prose: For any blue point \(p_0 = (x_b, y_b)\), green point \((x_g, y_g)\), and red point \((x_r, y_r)\), the \(v\)-value of the determinant \[ \det \begin{bmatrix} x_b & y_b & 1 \\ x_g & y_g & 1 \\ x_r & y_r & 1 \end{bmatrix} \] is at least \(1\).

label: ch22corollary
prose: Any line of the plane receives at most two different colors. The area of a rainbow triangle cannot be \(0\), and it cannot be \(\frac{1}{n}\) for odd \(n\).

label: ch22lemma2
prose: Every dissection of the unit square \(S = [0, 1]^2\) into finitely many triangles contains an odd number of rainbow triangles, and thus at least one.

label: monsky_theorem
prose: It is not possible to dissect a square into an odd number of triangles of equal algebra area.

label: valuation_lemma
prose: A proper subring \(R\subset K\) is a valuation ring with respect to some valuation \(v\) into some ordered group \(G\) if and only if \(K = R \cup R^{-1}\).

label: valuation_on_reals
prose: The field of real numbers \(\mathbb {R}\) has a non-Archimedean valuation to an ordered abelian group \[ v: \mathbb {R} \to \{ 0\} \cup G \] such that \(v(\frac{1}{2}) {\gt} 1\).

label: ch23theorem1
prose: Let \(f(z)\) be a complex polynomial of degree at least 1 and leading coefficient 1. Set \(C = \{ z \in \mathbb {C} : |f(z)| \leq 2 \} \) and let \(\mathcal{R}\) be the orthogonal projection of \(C\) onto the real axis. Then there are intervals \(I_1, \dots , I_t\) on the real line which together cover \(\mathcal{R}\) and satisfy \[ \ell (I_1) + \cdots + \ell (I_t) \leq 4. \]

label: ch23theorem2
prose: Let \(p(x)\) be a real polynomial of degree \(n \geq 1\) with leading coefficient 1, and all roots real. Then the set \(\mathcal{P} = \{ x \in \mathbb {R} : |p(x)| \leq 2\} \) can be covered by intervals of total length at most 4.

label: ch23corollary
prose: Let \(p(x)\) be a real polynomial of degree \(n \geq 1\) with leading coefficient 1, and suppose that \(|p(x)| \leq 2\) for all \(x\) in the interval \([a, b]\). Then \(b - a \leq 4\).

label: chebyshev
prose: Let \(p(x)\) be a real polynomial of degree \(n \geq 1\) with leading coefficient 1. Then \[ \max _{-1 \leq x \leq 1} |p(x)| \geq \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}. \]

label: ch23fact1
prose: If \(b\) is a multiple root of \(p'(x)\), then \(b\) is also a root of \(p(x)\).

label: ch23fact2
prose: We have \(p'(x)^2 \geq p(x)p''(x)\) for all \(x \in \mathbb {R}\).

label: vanderwaerden
prose: Let \(M = (m_{ij})\) be a doubly stochastic \(n \times n\) matrix. Then \[ \operatorname{per}M \ge \frac{n!}{n^n} \] and equality holds if and only if \(m_{ij} = \frac{1}{n}\)

label: gurvit
prose: If \(p(x)\in \mathbb {R}_+[x_1, \dots , x_n]\) is a \(H\)-stable and homogeneous of degree \(n\), then either \(p'\cong 0\), or \(p'\) is \(H\)-stable and homogeneous of degree \(n - 1\). In either case \[ \operatorname{cap}(p') \ge \operatorname{cap}\cdot g(\deg _np). \]

label: ch25theorem
prose: Let \(a_1, \dots , a_n\) be vectors in \(\mathbb {R}^d\), each of length at least 1, and let \(R_1, \dots , R_k\) be \(k\) open regions of \(\mathbb {R}^d\), where \(|x - y| {\lt} 2\) for any \(x, y\) that lie in the same region \(R_i\). Then the number of linear combinations \(\sum _{i=1}^{n} \epsilon _i a_i\), \(\epsilon _i \in \{ 1, -1\} \), that can lie in the union \(\bigcup _i R_i\) of the regions is at most the sum of the \(k\) largest binomial coefficients \(\binom {n}{j}\). In particular, we get the bound \(\binom {\lfloor n/2 \rfloor }{n}\) for \(k = 1\).

label: ch26lemma_a
prose: The functions \(f\) and \(g\) are defined for all non-integral values and are continuous there.

label: ch26lemma_b
prose: Both \(f\) and \(g\) are periodic of period \(1\), that is \(f(x + 1) = f(x)\) and \(g(x + 1) = g(x)\) hold for all \(x\in \mathbb {R}\setminus \mathbb {Z}\).

label: ch26lemma_c
prose: Both \(f\) and \(g\) are odd functions, that is we have \(f(-x) = -f(x)\) and \(g(-x) = -g(x)\) for all \(x\in \mathbb {R}\setminus \mathbb {Z}\).

label: ch26lemma_d
prose: The two functions \(f\) and \(g\) satisfy the same functional equation: \(f(\frac{x}{2}) + f(\frac{x + 1}{2}) = 2f(x)\) and \(g(\frac{x}{2}) + g(\frac{x + 1}{2}) = gf(x)\).

label: ch26lemma_e
prose: By setting \(h(x) := 0\) for \(x \in \mathbb {Z}\), \(h\) becomes a continuous function on all of \(\mathbb {R}\) that shares the properties given in 26.2 , 26.3 , 26.4 .

label: ch26
prose: \[ \pi \cot {\pi x} = \frac{1}{x} + \sum _{n = 1}^\infty \left(\frac{1}{x + n} + \frac{1}{x - n}\right) \] for \(x\in \mathbb {R}\setminus \mathbb {Z}\).

label: buffon_needle
prose: If a short needle, of length \(\ell \), is dropped on paper that is ruled with equally spaced lines of distance \(d \geq \ell \), then the probability that the needle comes to lie in a position where it crosses one of the lines is exactly \[ p = \frac{2 \ell }{\pi d}. \]

label: tiling_rectangles1
prose: Whenever a rectangle is tiled by rectangles all of which have at least one side of integer length, then the tiled rectangle has at least one side of integer length.

label: tiling_rectangles2
prose: Whenever a rectangle is tiled by rectangles all of which have at least one side of integer length, then the tiled rectangle has at least one side of integer length.

label: tiling_rectangles3
prose: Whenever a rectangle is tiled by rectangles all of which have at least one side of integer length, then the tiled rectangle has at least one side of integer length.

label: ch31lemma
prose: Let \(\mathbb {Q} : \mathfrak {S}_n \longrightarrow \mathbb {R}\) be any probability distribution that defines a shuffling process \(\mathbb {Q}^*k\) with a strong uniform stopping rule whose stopping time is \(T\). Then for all \(k \geq 0\), \[ ||\mathbb {Q}^*k - \mathbb {U}|| \leq \text{Prob}[T {\gt} k]. \]

label: ch31theorem1
prose: Let \(c \geq 0\) and \(k := \lceil n \log n + cn \rceil \). Then after performing \(k\) top-in-at-random shuffles on a deck of \(n\) cards, the variation distance from the uniform distribution satisfies \[ d(k) := ||\text{Top}^*k - \mathbb {U}|| \leq e^{-c}. \]

label: ch31theorem2
prose: After performing \(k\) riffle shuffles on a deck of \(n\) cards, the variation distance from a uniform distribution satisfies \[ ||\text{Rif}^*k - \mathbb {U}|| \leq 1 - \prod _{i=1}^{n-1} \left( 1 - \frac{i}{2^k} \right). \]

label: ch32lemma
prose: Let \(G = (V, E)\) be a finite weighted acyclic directed graph, \(A = \{ A_1, \dots , A_n\} \) and \(\mathcal{B} = \{ B_1, \dots , B_n\} \) two \(n\)-sets of vertices, and \(M\) the path matrix from \(A\) to \(\mathcal{B}\). Then \[ \det M = \sum _{\mathcal{P} \text{ vertex-disjoint path system}} \text{sign}(\mathcal{P}) \, w(\mathcal{P}). \tag {3} \]

label: ch32theorem
prose: Let \(G = (V, E)\) be a finite weighted acyclic directed graph, \(A = \{ A_1, \dots , A_n\} \) and \(\mathcal{B} = \{ B_1, \dots , B_n\} \) two \(n\)-sets of vertices, and \(M\) the path matrix from \(A\) to \(\mathcal{B}\). Then \[ \det M = \sum _{\mathcal{P} \text{ vertex-disjoint path system}} \text{sign}(\mathcal{P}) \, w(\mathcal{P}). \tag {3} \]

label: cayley_formala_proof1
prose: There are \(n^{n - 2}\) different labeled trees on \(n\) nodes.

label: cayley_formala_proof2
prose: There are \(n^{n - 2}\) different labeled trees on \(n\) nodes.

label: cayley_formala_proof3
prose: There are \(n^{n - 2}\) different labeled trees on \(n\) nodes.

label: cayley_formala_proof4
prose: There are \(n^{n - 2}\) different labeled trees on \(n\) nodes.

label: ch35lemma1
prose: Every nonzero polynomial \(p(x) \in F[x_1, \dots , x_n]\) of degree \(d\) has at most \(dq^{n-1}\) roots in \(F^n\).

label: ch35lemma2
prose: For every set \(E \subseteq F^n\) of size \(|E| {\lt} \binom {n+d}{d}\) there is a nonzero polynomial \(p(x) \in F[x_1, \dots , x_n]\) of degree at most \(d\) that vanishes on \(E\).

label: kakeya
prose: Let \(K \subseteq F^n\) be a Kakeya set. Then \[ |K| \geq \binom {|F| + n - 1}{n} \geq \frac{|F|^n}{n!}. \]

label: ch36lemma1
prose: Any \((r \times n)\)-Latin rectangle, \(r {\lt} n\), can be extended to an \(((r+1) \times n)\)-Latin rectangle and hence can be completed to a Latin square.

label: ch36lemma2
prose: Let \(P\) be a partial Latin square of order \(n\) with at most \(n - 1\) cells filled and at most \(\frac{n}{2}\) distinct elements, then \(P\) can be completed to a Latin square of order \(n\).

label: smetaniuk_theorem
prose: Any partial Latin square of order \(n\) with at most \(n - 1\) filled cells can be completed to a Latin square of the same order.

label: ch37theorem1
prose: Let \(M = (m_{ij})\) be an \(n \times n\) matrix with entries in \(\{ 0, 1\} \), and let \(d_1, \dots , d_n\) be the row sums of \(M\), that is, \(d_i = \sum _{j=1}^{n} m_{ij}\). Then \[ \operatorname {per} M \leq \prod _{i=1}^{n} (d_i!)^{1/d_i}. \]

label: ch37theorem2
prose: The number \(L(n)\) of Latin squares of order \(n\) is bounded by \[ \frac{n!^{2n}}{n^{n^2}} \le L(n) \le \prod _{k=1}^{n}k!^{n/k} \]

label: ch37fact_a
prose: \[ H(X)\le \log _2(|\operatorname{supp}{X}). \]

label: ch37fact_c
prose: \[ H(Y|X)\le \sum _{j=1}^d \operatorname{Prop}(X\in E_j)\log _2 j. \]

label: ch38definition1
prose: Let \(\vec{G} = (V, E)\) be a directed graph. A kernel \(K \subseteq V\) is a subset of the vertices such that \(K\) is independent in \(G\), and for every \(u \notin K\) there exists a vertex \(v \in K\) with an edge \(u \rightarrow v\).

label: ch38lemma1
prose: Let \(\vec{G} = (V, E)\) be a directed graph, and suppose that for each vertex \(v \in V\) we have a color set \(C(v)\) that is larger than the outdegree, \(|C(v)| \geq d^+(v) + 1\). If every induced subgraph of \(\vec{G}\) possesses a kernel, then there exists a list coloring of \(G\) with a color from \(C(v)\) for each \(v\).

label: ch38definition2
prose: A matching \(M\) of \(G = (X \cup Y, E)\) is called stable if the following condition holds: Whenever \(uv \in E \setminus M\), \(u \in X\), \(v \in Y\), then either \(uy \in M\) with \(y {\gt} v\) in \(N(u)\) or \(xv \in M\) with \(x {\gt} u\) in \(N(v)\), or both.

label: ch38lemma2
prose: A stable matching always exists.

label: ch38theorem
prose: We have \(\chi _\ell (S_n) = n\) for all \(n\).

label: five_colorable
prose: All planar graphs \(G\) can be \(5\)-colored: \[ \chi _{\ell }(G)\le 5. \]

label: museum_guards
prose: For any museum with \(n\) walls, \(\lfloor \frac{n}{3} \rfloor \) guards suffice.

label: ch41proof1
prose: If a graph \(G = (V, E)\) on \(n\) vertices has no \(p\)-clique, \(p \geq 2\), then \[ |E| \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{p - 1}\right) \frac{n^2}{2}. \tag {1} \]

label: ch41proof2
prose: If a graph \(G = (V, E)\) on \(n\) vertices has no \(p\)-clique, \(p \geq 2\), then \[ |E| \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{p - 1}\right) \frac{n^2}{2}. \tag {1} \]

label: ch41proof3
prose: If a graph \(G = (V, E)\) on \(n\) vertices has no \(p\)-clique, \(p \geq 2\), then \[ |E| \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{p - 1}\right) \frac{n^2}{2}. \tag {1} \]

label: ch41proof4
prose: If a graph \(G = (V, E)\) on \(n\) vertices has no \(p\)-clique, \(p \geq 2\), then \[ |E| \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{p - 1}\right) \frac{n^2}{2}. \tag {1} \]

label: ch41proof5
prose: If a graph \(G = (V, E)\) on \(n\) vertices has no \(p\)-clique, \(p \geq 2\), then \[ |E| \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{p - 1}\right) \frac{n^2}{2}. \tag {1} \]

label: turan_graph
prose: Collecting the proofs from the chapter...

label: ch42theorem
prose: Whenever \(T = \{ v^{(1)}, \dots , v^{(m)}\} \) is an orthonormal representation of \(G\) with constant \(\sigma _T\), then \[ \Theta (G) \leq \frac{1}{\sigma _T}. \]

label: lyusternik_shnirelman
prose: If the \(d\)-sphere \(S^d\) is covered by \(d + 1\) sets, \[ S^d = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_d \cup U_{d+1}, \] such that each of the first \(d\) sets \(U_1, \dots , U_d\) is either open or closed, then one of the \(d + 1\) sets contains a pair of antipodal points \(x^*, -x^*\).

label: gale_theorem
prose: There is an arrangement of \(2k + d\) points on \(S^d\) such that every open hemisphere contains at least \(k\) of these points.

label: kneser_conjecture
prose: We have \[ \chi (K(2k + d, k)) = d + 2. \]

label: borsuk_ulam
prose: For every continuous map \(f : S^d \to \mathbb {R}^d\) from \(d\)-sphere to \(d\)-space, there are antipodal points \(x^*\), \(-x^*\) that are mapped to the same point \(f(x^*) = f(-x^*)\).

label: friendship
prose: Suppose that \(G\) is a finite graph in which any two vertices have precisely one common neighbor. Then there is a vertex which is adjacent to all other vertices.

label: ch45theorem1
prose: Every family of at most \(2^{d-1}\) \(d\)-sets is \(2\)-colorable, that is, \(m(d) {\gt} 2^{d-1}\).

label: ch45theorem2
prose: Every family of at most \(2^{d-1}\) \(d\)-sets is \(2\)-colorable, that is, \(m(d) {\gt} 2^{d-1}\).

label: ch45theorem3
prose: For every \(k \geq 2\), there exists a graph \(G\) with chromatic number \(\chi (G) {\gt} k\) and girth \(\gamma (G) {\gt} k\).

label: ch45theorem4
prose: Let \(G\) be a simple graph with \(n\) vertices and \(m\) edges, where \(m \ge 4n\). Then \[ \operatorname{cr}(G) \ge \frac{1}{64}\frac{m^3}{n^2}. \]
